name: Politics & Government
description: Test your knowledge of world leaders, elections, and governance.

questions.text: Who was the first President of the United States?
questions.explanation: George Washington served as the first U.S. President from 1789 to 1797.

questions.text: Which country has a government system known as a constitutional monarchy?
questions.explanation: The United Kingdom is a constitutional monarchy where the monarch's powers are limited by a constitution and Parliament.

questions.text: The United Nations was founded in which year?
questions.explanation: The United Nations was founded on October 24, 1945, after World War II to promote international peace and cooperation.

questions.text: Who wrote the Communist Manifesto?
questions.explanation: Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels co-authored The Communist Manifesto, published in 1848.

questions.text: Which body is the upper house of the U.S. Congress?
questions.explanation: The U.S. Senate is the upper chamber of Congress, with two senators per state.

questions.text: NATO stands for which organization?
questions.explanation: NATO stands for the North Atlantic Treaty Organization, a military alliance founded in 1949.

questions.text: Which country is the world's largest democracy by population?
questions.explanation: India is the world's largest democracy with over 1.4 billion people and universal adult suffrage.

questions.text: Who was the first female Prime Minister of the United Kingdom?
questions.explanation: Margaret Thatcher became the UK's first female Prime Minister in 1979 and served until 1990.

questions.text: What is the term length of a U.S. President?
questions.explanation: U.S. Presidents serve four-year terms and may be re-elected for a maximum of two terms.

questions.text: The European Union's headquarters is located in which city?
questions.explanation: Brussels, Belgium hosts the main institutions of the European Union.

questions.text: Which amendment to the U.S. Constitution abolished slavery?
questions.explanation: The 13th Amendment, ratified in 1865, abolished slavery throughout the United States.

questions.text: What is the name of the lower house of the UK Parliament?
questions.explanation: The House of Commons is the elected lower house of the UK Parliament.

questions.text: Which country held the world's first women's suffrage at national level in 1893?
questions.explanation: New Zealand became the first country to grant women the right to vote in national elections in 1893.

questions.text: How many permanent members does the UN Security Council have?
questions.explanation: The UN Security Council has 5 permanent members: the US, UK, France, Russia, and China.

questions.text: What is the name of France's legislative body?
questions.explanation: France's lower house of parliament is called the National Assembly (Assemblée nationale).

questions.text: Which political system has a single ruler with absolute power?
questions.explanation: An autocracy is a political system where one person holds unrestricted political power.

questions.text: Who is considered the father of modern conservatism?
questions.explanation: Edmund Burke, an 18th-century Irish-British statesman, is widely regarded as the father of modern conservatism.

questions.text: Which country adopted the world's first written constitution in 1787?
questions.explanation: The United States Constitution, drafted in 1787 and ratified in 1788, is the world's oldest written national constitution still in use.

questions.text: The Magna Carta was signed in which year?
questions.explanation: King John of England signed the Magna Carta in 1215, limiting royal power and establishing rule of law.

questions.text: Which organization replaced the League of Nations after World War II?
questions.explanation: The United Nations replaced the failed League of Nations in 1945 to maintain international peace.

questions.text: What is the term for a government controlled by a small group of people?
questions.explanation: An oligarchy is a form of government where power rests with a small, elite group.

questions.text: Which treaty ended World War I?
questions.explanation: The Treaty of Versailles, signed in 1919, officially ended World War I and imposed heavy reparations on Germany.

questions.text: Which country has the largest number of seats in the European Parliament?
questions.explanation: Germany has the most seats in the European Parliament due to its large population.

questions.text: Who was the first Secretary-General of the United Nations?
questions.explanation: Trygve Lie of Norway served as the first UN Secretary-General from 1946 to 1952.

questions.text: What political system does China officially practice?
questions.explanation: China officially describes its system as 'Socialism with Chinese characteristics,' a unique blend of socialist principles and market economics.

questions.text: Which U.S. President signed the Civil Rights Act of 1964?
questions.explanation: President Lyndon B. Johnson signed the Civil Rights Act of 1964, prohibiting discrimination based on race, color, religion, or national origin.

questions.text: The Kyoto Protocol was primarily concerned with which issue?
questions.explanation: The Kyoto Protocol (1997) required industrialized nations to reduce greenhouse gas emissions to combat climate change.

questions.text: Which country is NOT a member of BRICS?
questions.explanation: Indonesia is not a founding member of BRICS; the original group consists of Brazil, Russia, India, China, and South Africa.

questions.text: Who coined the term 'Iron Curtain' in his famous 1946 speech?
questions.explanation: Winston Churchill used the phrase 'Iron Curtain' in his 1946 Fulton, Missouri speech to describe the Soviet divide of Europe.

questions.text: Which amendment gave U.S. women the right to vote?
questions.explanation: The 19th Amendment, ratified in 1920, granted American women the right to vote.

questions.text: What is the political ideology that advocates for individual freedom from government interference?
questions.explanation: Libertarianism emphasizes individual rights and minimal government intervention in personal and economic affairs.

questions.text: Which country was the first to legalize same-sex marriage nationally?
questions.explanation: The Netherlands became the first country in the world to legalize same-sex marriage in 2001.

questions.text: What is a 'filibuster' in legislative terms?
options: A procedural tactic to delay or block legislation
questions.explanation: A filibuster is a tactic where senators extend debate to delay or prevent a vote on legislation.

questions.text: Which political theorist wrote 'The Social Contract'?
questions.explanation: Jean-Jacques Rousseau wrote 'The Social Contract' (1762), arguing that legitimate government derives from a collective agreement among citizens.

questions.text: What is the name of the legislative body in Germany?
questions.explanation: The Bundestag is Germany's federal parliament, the primary legislative body of the Federal Republic of Germany.

questions.text: Which doctrine established U.S. policy to contain Soviet expansion during the Cold War?
questions.explanation: The Truman Doctrine (1947) committed the U.S. to containing Soviet influence, starting with aid to Greece and Turkey.

questions.text: Which Indian Prime Minister declared a national emergency in 1975?
questions.explanation: Indira Gandhi declared a state of emergency in India from 1975 to 1977, suspending civil liberties and democratic processes.

questions.text: The Westphalian system of state sovereignty originated from which treaty?
questions.explanation: The Peace of Westphalia (1648) established the principle of state sovereignty that underpins modern international relations.

questions.text: What is the political concept of 'Realpolitik' associated with?
questions.explanation: Realpolitik is a pragmatic approach to politics and diplomacy based on practical power considerations rather than ideological principles.

questions.text: Which country was the first to grant women the right to vote in a national election in Europe?
questions.explanation: Finland was the first European country to grant women full political rights including the right to vote and stand for election, in 1906.

questions.text: The concept of 'Checks and Balances' in government was most famously advocated by which philosopher?
questions.explanation: Montesquieu's 'The Spirit of the Laws' (1748) popularized the separation of powers and checks and balances among government branches.

questions.text: Which international agreement established the rules for the conduct of war and protection of civilians?
questions.explanation: The Geneva Conventions are a series of international treaties establishing standards for humanitarian treatment in war.

questions.text: What is 'Gerrymandering'?
options: Manipulating electoral district boundaries for political advantage
questions.explanation: Gerrymandering is the manipulation of electoral district boundaries to favor a particular political party or group.

questions.text: Which political theorist is associated with the concept of 'Leviathan' as a metaphor for the state?
questions.explanation: Thomas Hobbes used 'Leviathan' in his 1651 work to describe a powerful sovereign state necessary to prevent societal chaos.

questions.text: Which country first implemented proportional representation in parliamentary elections?
questions.explanation: Belgium introduced proportional representation in 1899, becoming one of the first countries to implement this electoral system.

questions.text: The Non-Aligned Movement was founded at the 1961 Belgrade Conference by which leader?
questions.explanation: Josip Broz Tito of Yugoslavia was a key founder and host of the 1961 Belgrade Conference that established the Non-Aligned Movement.

questions.text: What is the 'Overton Window' in political science?
options: The range of policies politically acceptable to the mainstream
A type of government transparency law
questions.explanation: The Overton Window describes the range of ideas considered politically acceptable or mainstream at a given time.

questions.text: Which treaty created the European Economic Community (EEC) in 1957?
questions.explanation: The Treaty of Rome (1957) established the European Economic Community, the precursor to today's European Union.

questions.text: Which U.S. President issued Executive Order 9066, authorizing Japanese American internment during WWII?
questions.explanation: Franklin D. Roosevelt signed Executive Order 9066 in 1942, leading to the forced internment of over 120,000 Japanese Americans.

questions.text: The concept of 'democratic peace theory' suggests what?
options: Democracies rarely go to war with each other
questions.explanation: Democratic peace theory holds that liberal democracies rarely or never wage war against each other, though they may fight non-democracies.
